chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing borderline MUAC and WHZ values in outpatient care, emphasizing technical accuracy, clinical risk stratification, and prioritization for dietary support based on WHO risk factors.
learning_objectives: Understand the significance of MUAC and WHZ discordance in malnutrition risk assessment.
Learn technical steps to confirm anthropometric measurements and ensure accuracy.
Identify WHO risk factors for prioritizing children for intensive dietary support and follow-up.
Apply safe management practices for children with borderline malnutrition metrics.
key_facts: MUAC borderline values: 124–125 mm; WHZ near -2 indicate overlapping risk phenotypes.
Mortality risk is highest when both MUAC and WHZ are low.
WHO risk factors include MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age < 24 months, and comorbidities like HIV or TB.
Technical confirmation involves repeating measurements, calibrating equipment, and ensuring proper technique.
Children with borderline metrics should be managed 'as if eligible' for intensified support when uncertainty persists.
reference_quote: Mortality risk is highest when both MUAC and WHZ are low. (PLOS ONE, MUAC and WHZ as predictors of mortality and programme outcomes in children under 5 years)
document_key_topics: Management of borderline MUAC and WHZ values
WHO risk factors for malnutrition prioritization
Clinical strategies for discordant metrics in outpatient care
